justifications.ip1: All three ACs have distinct, specific failing test scenarios: AC1 covered by T1 (channel '#aml-alerts') and T2 (alertId/amount in message), AC2 by T3 (correct to/subject) and T4 (alertId in body), AC3 by T5 (slack fail → {slack:'failed',email:'sent'}) and T6 (both fail → resolves, never throws).
justifications.ip2: No tasks implement SAR filing, MLRO escalation, alert deduplication, threshold detection, or compliance UI; the architecture constraint to use 'src/audit/audit-logger.js' (not console.log) is explicitly enforced via the factory injection pattern and documented as a tech lead review gate.
justifications.ip3: Every task names specific files ('tests/compliance/alert-router.test.js', 'src/compliance/alert-router.js'), concrete function signatures ('createAlertRouter(slackClient, emailTransport, auditLog)', 'routeAlert(payload)'), and precise test assertions (e.g. 'expect(slack.chat.postMessage).toHaveBeenCalledWith(expect.objectContaining({ channel: '#aml-alerts' }))') leaving no ambiguity for a subagent.
justifications.ip4: TASK 1 creates all 8 failing tests before any implementation exists ('No implementation file exists yet, so every require will fail — confirming RED'), TASK 2 then writes the minimum implementation to GREEN, with explicit RED and GREEN state confirmations stated after each task.
justifications.ip5: NFR-1 (audit logging) generates two dedicated test scenarios T7 (auditLog.log called exactly once) and T8 (log entry contains alertId, timestamp, channel statuses), and the architecture constraint ('MUST use src/audit/audit-logger.js — NOT console.log') is enforced as an explicit task-level implementation rule via the factory pattern with a tech lead checkpoint.

notes: Exemplary plan: all ACs have precise failing test scenarios, the audit-logger constraint is enforced structurally via dependency injection rather than just documented, and the TDD RED→GREEN flow is unambiguous throughout.
